names.common_name: Diacetyl Tartaric Acid Esters of Mono- and Diglycerides
names.japanese_name: ジアセチル酒石酸モノグリセリド
names.japanese_label_name: グリセリン脂肪酸エステル

natural_occurrence.description: Synthetic emulsifier created by esterifying mono- and diglycerides with diacetyl tartaric acid. This is one of the most widely used emulsifiers in commercial bread production worldwide - found in almost all supermarket bread, rolls, and buns.

manufacturing.description: Produced by reacting mono- and diglycerides (derived from vegetable oils or animal fats) with diacetyl tartaric acid (a derivative of tartaric acid). The process requires controlled heating and esterification catalysts.

eu.conditions: Approved for use at quantum satis in specified food categories; extremely common in commercial bread

usa.notes: GRAS status; ubiquitous in commercial bakery products

japan.japanese_regulation_name: ジアセチル酒石酸モノグリセリド

australia_nz.notes: Approved as emulsifier; widely used in commercial bread

canada.reference: Lists of Permitted Emulsifying, Gelling, Stabilizing or Thickening Agents
canada.notes: Permitted in various food categories; standard in commercial baking

regulatory_history.event: JECFA evaluates diacetyl tartaric acid esters of mono- and diglycerides
Becomes standard emulsifier in commercial bread production
EU regulation codifies E472e in Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008
EFSA re-evaluates mono- and diglycerides and their esters, confirms safety

adi_context.source_note: No ADI specified - considered safe for use at quantum satis levels. DATEM is the workhorse emulsifier of the commercial baking industry, strengthening gluten, improving dough handling, and extending shelf life. If you eat commercial bread, you're almost certainly consuming DATEM daily.
